You've heard it all before. The stock market average return is 8 to 10 percent, but is that what ends up in your pocket? Investing in the stock market is not a clear-cut case of banner headline returns. Taxes rebalancing and inflation can claw back some of the shining promises made by historical averages. But with products and strategies like annuities you can navigate around some of these profit-eating traps. investing goal.

## NC0007 C0AIa7DSY6d

Source URL: https://www.instagram.com/nicholascrown/reel/C0AIa7DSY6d/

With index annuities there's something called a participation rate. It essentially tells you how much of the return from the stock market or whatever index the insurance company is going to use to calculate your rate of return. Let me give you a simple example. Let's say your annuity has a 70% participation rate. And the benchmark index, the S&P 500, goes up by 10%. That means your rate of return would be about 7%.

---

## NC0009 C0KcO0VpcHQ

Source URL: https://www.instagram.com/nicholascrown/reel/C0KcO0VpcHQ/

The traditional IRA. IRA. IRA just stands for individual retirement account. This is like a Swiss Army knife in a tax advantage toolkit. With an IRA you don't need an employer to sponsor the plan. In fact, you can deduct your contributions now, meaning pre-tax dollars. Your investment earnings get to chill out, and they stay tax deferred until you're ready to retire. But there's a catch. There is a contribution limit to the amount that you could put in each year.

---

## NC0010 C0O8gietZM_

Source URL: https://www.instagram.com/nicholascrown/reel/C0O8gietZM_/

Unlike 401k's and traditional IRAs, fixed and index annuities provide a straightforward path. With a 401k, you are the captain of your investment journey, which some people like. But with an annuity you're more like a passenger on a luxury liner. So where the Roth IRA offers some tax advantages, which is undoubtedly attractive if you don't earn your way out of the Roth, annuities brings something else to table, which is guaranteed income.

---

## NC0011 C0PmKlEsT11

Source URL: https://www.instagram.com/nicholascrown/reel/C0PmKlEsT11/

And to zoom in on index annuities, which bridge the gap between fixed annuities and the ugly dirty cousin called variable annuities, which I'm not even to talk about in this video. They provide more upside by linking your returns to an index like the S&P 500, while maintaining a safety net, meaning you can earn up to 8 or 10%, but you can never earn less than 0.

Options. Options are financial derivatives, derivatives meaning they derive their value from something else like an underlying stock. Options are the Japanese blowfish of investing. A delicacy if you know what you're doing, and deadly if you don't. Like all tools, they can be used conservatively to preserve value or hedge a portfolio against losses, or on the flip side to speculate and potentially send you to the poorhouse. Keep in mind that the big players and options are some of the smartest people in the business, with extremely powerful pricing models and simulations backing them up. don't try to beat them at their own game. However, here's how a beginner could use options intelligently in the current market environment. Now, there are two types of options available, puts and calls. If you were to buy a call, you're making a bet that the price of a call, you're making a bet that the price of something will decrease. For the right to an option, you got to pay a price, it's called a premium. And like all markets, if you're buying something is buying you, it's, price it's all connected. Now let's say you own some stock and Big Bubbleco price at a hundred bucks a share. This is a fake company of course and you're worried about a big price decrease if the Federal Reserve raises interest rates higher than expected. You could head your investment in Big bubbleco by buying some $90 puts. If the Fed hikes big and the stock drops below 90, you'll make up for the price putts increasing. 90 bucks is called the strike price by the way. And bingo you didn't before hurricane season. And just like an insurance policy, there's an expiration date on an option. So you want to make sure you buy puts that don't expire before the big event that you're trying to protect against. Look, options are particularly complicated due to the many factors that go into their pricing, so they're not for everyone. But viewing them as strictly hedging tools is a very good start to understanding derivatives..

## NC0916 DG3L93YsaSG

Source URL: https://www.instagram.com/nicholascrown/reel/DG3L93YsaSG/

there is a subtle but very important difference between buying the dip and dollar cost averaging. Buying the dip is a discretionary trade masked as value investing and it's one where the buyer has made three false assumptions. One that it's only a dip meaning it's a small price dislocation. Two that it's temporary or at the end of a downward cycle and three that whatever price you're buying is a good one for this security. As you can see, buying the dip is a short-term directional his purchase is wrong and is more likely to buy before a larger downturn move not a rebound now dollar cost averaging is a program trade meaning it's scheduled in advance and you must follow it religiously let's say every month on a certain business day that means buying a security or index no matter if the price is better or worse this is not a discretionary trade rather it allows the investor to reduce timing risk and potentially very little. Other than that owning a diversified portfolio is probably a good idea in the long run.

I remember when I went to Chipotle hung over on a weekend to get a burrito and to feel better about myself. I saw McNeil sitting there the written format and then that hot shot Wall Street language from McNeil and put it together, that's what the Crown macro letter is. I'm trying to give you guys the tools that just don't exist anymore. And quite frankly, I'm trying to give myself the fun and entertainment of building this letter for myself, letting me think deeper about trades. Prediction markets are bullshit, but serious traders are using them to make lots of money. And it's not by placing bets on Polymarket or call sheet. Prediction markets are an early way to see positioning. You see what the crowd is willing to pay for before the headline, before the data, and sometimes before the broader market moves. One example of this was the Iran war. If you look at the data, there were late trades placed on Polymarket, hours before the Iran strikes were announced. and so early that the platforms are now under investigation and they're refusing to pay out the winnings using the prediction markets as your underlying bet is inherently foolish because of the thin liquidity and the insider bias. So are prediction markets yes if you're trading on prediction markets, but they can heavily influence your bets in the real market?

Leveraged EDFs are combinations of swaps, futures, and financing. The biggest silent killer in leveraged EDFs is something called volatility drag. For instance, if the market goes down 10%, you need to make up 11% just to pay for the costs associated with rebalancing. With a leveraged EDF, you are not buying leverage, you are renting it. I wish I knew this when I just started trading, but price is a lagging indicator. And its positioning data that really matters. Let me explain. And it's not just one source, it's three sources of positioning data that traders use to get ahead of every move. And if you master these three, you can literally operate like a hedge fund. The first is the CFTC cot, and that stands for the commitment of traders. I'll drop a screenshot right here so you can see it. it covers 20 trillion dollars in futures and this is everything from agricultural commodities to nat gas to crude oil metals and most importantly the e-minny S&P future contract. The CFTC cot does a really great job of telling you when a position is getting crowded or still has room to run. Number two is prime brokerage data. Goldman Sachs prime brokerage data is like the holy grail. Here Goldman tracks every hedge fund exposure, net exposure, and even sector tilts. So this thing gets pretty granular. And this isn't just some cheesy survey they send to hedge fund managers. This is the actual data. And this data goes out to Goldman's institutional clients, and it occasionally gets leaked. If you have several banks prime brokerage data in your hands, you can spot a short squeeze from a mile away. Number three is dealer gamma, and this is the most important real-time data. of every single institutional and retail trade. So when dealers are short gamma, moves can get, well, exaggerated. As dealers often join a rally or keep selling into a falling market. And when dealers are long gamma, moves are suppressed, and gamma buildup can act like a magnet, have to compile all this data together. In isolation these data points can be noise. Now the CFTC data you can pull that yourself but for everything else I roll it into the crown macro letter so you have a composite picture of positioning. Gold is the most inefficient store of value on earth. But that doesn't stop central banks from fighting over it for this one reason. Let me explain. Gold is the only reserve asset that cannot be defaulted on, frozen or sanctioned by another country. For instance, dollars sit in accounts at the Federal Reserve, euros, sit in accounts at the ECB. Even US treasuries have to sit at a custodian bank.!!!!!!! very own vault, no one gets to tell you what to do. But because of its inefficiency and cost, it's the asset you hold for the moments, the other stuff stops working. And this is why Central Banks bought that every central banker's worst nightmare came true. This is when 300 billion dollars of Russian bank reserves were frozen overnight. And it's not just the richest countries in the world buying gold to avoid this Russian scenario, it's all the countries. Poland bought 31 tons last quarter, Uzbekistan bought 25, and even Kazakhstan added 13 tons. When every reserve manager sees that dollar-denominated assets can be toggled on and off, they want more gold. geopolitically driven market. So as you can see, a country's independence is measured in tons. And when you, the investor, buys gold, you aren't buying a fear hedge. That's a cliche. What used to be a reliable hedge for stocks is now double risk. That's because gold's correlation with equities flipped in February. And it's important that you understand this so you know what to do next. From May through October 2025, Gold and the S&P 500 had a daily correlation of negative spot to 7. This window is classic hedge behavior. Now by December of last year, both the 60 and 90-day windows started to roll positive. is starting to break down. And since just last month the correlation is now positive spot to two. That means that gold is not offsetting equity risk anymore. It's moving with stocks, it's trading like a risk asset, which means the market is pricing gold as a cyclical reflation asset and not as portfolio insurance. However that doesn't mean that gold goes down in isolation. It means that if stocks fall hard, gold is coming with it. And this is all happening because we are in a war regime. last forever. But looking at where we are in the timeline, this could take many, many months. And that's just long enough for you to reassess why you own gold. So the trade right now is not whether gold goes up or down. The trade is understanding exactly what gold is doing inside your portfolio. Positioning tells you what's possible. So for the copper example, you could pull the CFTC cot, and after reading the report, you see that managed money is 90% net long. Well, guess the trade is 90% percent. We look at the three-month implied vol versus realized vol. We look at skew, calls versus puts, because if implied vol is at the 80th percentile and call skew is bid, the market is already paying for that breakout. So if you were to buy calls, you'd be paying a premium for a move that has already happened in volatility. And step four, and don't forget this one, this is where people get run over, is check the event calendar. You've got to know what's on the calendar between now and when you plan to exit the trade. U.S. manufacturing data. I wish I knew this when I was just starting out, but buying a call option can be the worst way to bet on a stock going up. And that's because most options traders make the same mistake. And that's using the textbook definition. If you're bullish, you're buying puts. And that feels logical, but it is incomplete. And it's certainly not how professionals think, you need to know. An IV rank tells you where today's implied volatility sits within its own 12 month range. And there's two sides of the scale you want to remember. Above 70, options are expensive and below 30. Options are cheap. And that one number changes the entire trade. So let's just say you're bullish, and the IV rank is 85. That means you're paying three times as much premium just to buy the call. And in that case, you might be right on direction. The stock might rip, but you can still lose as volatility and this is happens all the time after big events like earnings. Now let's just say you're bullish and IV rank is 20. This is a totally different trade. And here buying calls might actually make sense because the option is relatively cheap. That's why professionals don't ask, am I bullish or am I bearish? They ask what is the market charging me to express my view. So all else being equal, if vol is cheap, buy options.!!!! want a list of my pre-structured and pre-filtered trades I do this all for you every single week just comment letter below and I'll send you the link

## NC1745 DYVHysKDVzu

Source URL: https://www.instagram.com/nicholascrown/reel/DYVHysKDVzu/

I wish I knew this when I started trading, but price is a lagging indicator, and its positioning that really matters. And it's not one source, it's three sources of positioning data that traders use to get ahead of every move. The first is the CFTC caught, I just showed you on my screen right there. The second is prime brokerage data, and the third is dealer gamma. If you master these three, you can literally operate like a hedge fund. So first is the COT,! The first is the COT, from Ag's to Net Gas to crude oil and yes E-Many futures. This tells you when a trade is getting crowded or still has room to run. Number two is Prime Brokerage Data. Goldman Sachs Prime Brokerge data is like the holy grail. They track every hedge fund book they finance, and this is gross exposure, net exposure, sector tilt and even factor names. This stuff is granular. And it's not some cheesy survey survey the email to hedge fund managers.!!!!!!!!!!! Number three is dealer gamma. You have to keep in mind options dealers have the other side of every single institutional and retail options trade. So when dealers are short gamma moves can get amplified and when dealers are long gamma moves are suppressed and the gamma can act like a magnet. Positive gamma levels are the actual support and resistance in the market. The CFTC data you can pull it yourself for everything else I rolled into the the like.

## NC1748 DYXssgQgWDW

Source URL: https://www.instagram.com/nicholascrown/reel/DYXssgQgWDW/

Silver isn't always silver. Sometimes it's gold and sometimes it's copper. And if you don't know what I'm talking about, let me explain. Silver trades on different fundamentals depending on what regime we're in. When silver trades like gold, it's trading like a monetary hedge. That version is driven by real yields. It's driven by the dollar, inflation, anxiety, Central Bank credibility, and finally the demand for hard assets. But when silver trades like copper, silver becomes an industrial metal. applications, electrification, solar, and electronics, AI power infrastructure, and the broader base metals cycle. And right now, silver is not trading like gold. It's trading like copper. In fact, the 30-day return correlation between silver and copper is spot 66. And to make it even more interesting, the copper to gold ratio is up 13% over the last 30 days, and sitting at the top of its range. That's how much the bid for industrial metals is leading the way forward. That's not a bid for precious metals or just diversification. That is silver getting pulled into the industrial trade. So the question isn't, is silver going up or going down? It's which version of silver are we trading? The gold version of silver that's a monetary hedge, or the copper version of silver that reacts to industrial demand. If you know how to answer that question, well you know how to trade silver. Right now the market is saying silver is trading like copper. on metals every single week and get my most recent trade ideas. Just comment letter below and I'll send you the like.

## NC1751 DYas5yKAibv

Source URL: https://www.instagram.com/nicholascrown/reel/DYas5yKAibv/

There's one trading rule I've heard for years that I've never fully trusted. Don't short the S&P outright ever. If this rule is true, it means everything to a trader. So I tested it. Here's your trading golden rule. I looked at roughly 20 years of SPX data. That's about 5,000 trading days. And I ran this test from mid- 2006 to today, and then I split the two regimes. two ways that traders actually short the market. The first is top picking, so finding RSI above 70 and seeing price touch the upper Bollinger band, a trader sees this setup and thinks this is over-bought, it's time for me to sell it. And the second is a breakdown, so a close below the prior 20-day low and a cross below the 50-day moving average. This is what actual confirmed selling looks like. Then I measured the forward returns over 5, 10, 20, and 40 trading days. And the results were brutal. only had a 31 to 35% hit rate. In trading that's like taking a nap in front of a steam roller. Now here's the really crazy part. Even in a downtrend the short didn't become a better trade. In a bare market, shorting the market only gave you a 42% hit rate. So the buyer's hit rate is something around 60% no matter what the market is doing. A 60% hit rate isn't just improved odds, it's an edge. So the golden rule is real. and that's because drift works against you. But it doesn't mean you can't be bearish. You just have to express it a different way, specifically with options. If you want to see how I structure bearish trades and get away with it, I publish my setups every single week in the Crown Macro letter. Just head to my profile and click the link.

## NC1754 DYf6oEhDMgi

Source URL: https://www.instagram.com/nicholascrown/reel/DYf6oEhDMgi/

Let me tell you a little secret. The VIX is not the fear gauge. And the biggest mistake investors make is treating it like a cell signal. And that's because the VIX is not measuring fear at all. It simply reflects what the options market is charging for 30-day movement or volatility on the S&P 500. If you've heard traders talk about implied trading at 15. What that means is that the options market is pricing in 15% annualized volatility over the next 30 days. So when Vicks jumps to 20 or even 25, the cost of protection on the S&P 500 has increased. And it's not because the market feels afraid. It's just because investors are willing to pay more for uncertainty and protection. And this is why the Vicks can fall even as market news looks bad. This is because the market was already hedged. a disaster and that hedge starts to get unwound. So as you can see, VIX is not a motion. It's just the price of implied volatility. And once you understand that, you're going to stop thinking, is the market scared and you're going to start asking is protection cheap or expensive? If you want to see how I use VIX to structure trades and hedge risk every single week, just comment letter below and I'll send you the link.

So when Invidia reports earnings, there is a chain reaction through the option surface. And it kind of goes like this. First, invidia re-price, then the entire mega-cap re-prises. And finally, all the hedges on the entire index re-price. So by the next morning when cash reopens, what started out is just one, companies earnings report has become the entire index wide dealer flow. And this is how a clean invidia beat can force dealers to chase the market higher and a disappointment causes them to bid for protection and puts pressure on the entire stock market. Now options market makers generally price in a 6-7% implied move as we head into invidia earnings. So invidia doesn't just have to beat. And let's just say you've got a reason to think this stock should be trading ten dollars. Maybe you did some research or your uncles in the it's not going to break out and you're going to head back down to five dollars again. So should you drink a lot of coffee stay up all night waiting for that breakout? Here's what I do. I place a very tiny stop buy order above the $8 level, let's say $8.20.20.00 level.00.00. or if you're you're you're you're you're a trade alert and this is critical this just acts as a signal I do not put the full trade on yet and that's because we need to close above this 820 level depending on your market or your time window this means a new candle is generated above that level I don't care how many times we tap eight dollars I need to see confirmation with a close this is how I know the market has accepted the higher level. Once that new new trailing stop. This is instant risk management. I know my entry and I know when to get out if this thing isn't working. Same day baby options worth pennies are controlling the entire stock market. Let me explain. In 2022, the CBOE released options that expire the same day and they called them zero DTE. So if you buy a zero DTE call option on the S&P 500, you're not just betting that the market's going to go up. You're betting that it goes up enough, fast enough before the closing bell. And that's why these options can trade for pennies, there is little time value embedded in the option. little guys aren't powerful. No one, not even the CBOE could have predicted this, but this product exploded. According to CBOE's own research, zero DTE options reached 62.4% of total SPX options volume in 2025. So zero DTE aren't some little tiny casino in the corner of the market. This is now becoming the market. And once something new like this becomes so large, it can start changing price behavior. same day calls. Guess what? A market maker is selling the calls, but the market makers aren't just going to sit there sitting naked short to upside. So they hedge the position with SPX futures. Let's just say the market starts rallying. Those zero-dte calls start becoming more sensitive because they expire today. And that sensitivity is called gamma. So in this case, the dealer might have to buy more futures, which can force more futures, which can force more As you can see, this tiny little option is like the first domino in a huge chain reaction. And famously the zero DTE options do two things really well. Number one, they can pin the market near a level, creating sticky price action on round handles like 7400, If you want to see how I use zero DTE options flow to uncover the best trades in the market, just comment letter below and I'll send you the link.

Selling moving average breaks and crosses don't work. Unless you check these three things first. Now let's use crude oil for an example. But this could be any asset. A technical break is just one input. And no matter what the technical analysts tell you, it is not the most important. And you're going to want to watch the whole video because these work together. The first is positioning. Think of positioning as who's! for crude or any asset that you're trading. When managed money is extremely net long and price starts to break down, you can see a very crowded trade start to unwind. This is when you see the bottom fall out of the market. But on the other hand, when net longs have already been reducing their position for weeks and the price starts to fall, there's really no fuel to pour on the fire. And that's when you just get chopped up and stopped out. hedgers are panicked. On the other hand, if you're entering a trade and put skew is just modest, the options market is telling you to do nothing. And the third is term structure. In the example, this is front month versus back month crude. A technical break with the curve in backredation is a very different trade than a curve in contango. In a backwardated market, there is still a huge amount of demand or a very tight supply. So a moving meaning there's some fuel to pour on the fire and longs to run towards the exit. The option skew should point too rich downside with hedgers paying up for puts. And finally, the term should support the direction of your trade. A heavily backwardated market is a very dangerous market to short. When one of these two point the other way you don't have a trade, you have a coin toss dressed up as technical analysis. comment, let her below, and I'll send you the link.
